Result: The proposed LG-TS-FFN framework is proven to exactly simulate AFA dynamics and to recover automaton structures and logic via gradient descent, achieving perfect performance in experiments.

Conclusion: This framework bridges statistical learning and logical reasoning by allowing neural networks to simulate computationally powerful automata like AFAs while maintaining succinctness and recoverability of logic structures.

Abstract: We present a formal and constructive framework for simulating Alternating Finite Automata (AFAs) using Logic-Gated Time-Shared Feedforward Networks (LG-TS-FFNs). Unlike prior neural automata models limited to Nondeterministic Finite Automata (NFAs) and existential reachability, our architecture integrates learnable, state-dependent biases that function as differentiable logic gates, enabling the representation of both Existential \textsc{\textsc{OR}} and Universal \textsc{\textsc{AND}} aggregation within a shared-parameter linear recurrence. We prove that this architectural modification upgrades the network's computational class to be structurally isomorphic to AFAs, thereby inheriting their exponential succinctness: the network can represent regular languages requiring $2^n$ states in an NFA with only $n$ neurons. We rigorously establish that the forward pass of an LG-TS-FFN exactly simulates the reachability dynamics of an AFA, including instantaneous $\varepsilon$-closures. Furthermore, we demonstrate empirical learnability: a continuous relaxation of the logic gates allows the network to simultaneously recover the automaton's topology and logical semantics from binary labels via standard gradient descent. Extensive experiments confirm that our model achieves perfect recovery of ground-truth automata, bridging the gap between statistical learning and succinct, universal logical reasoning.

</details>


### [444] [(PAC-)Learning state machines from data streams: A generic strategy and an improved heuristic (Extended version)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.02244)
*Robert Baumgartner,Sicco Verwer*

Main category: cs.FL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a method to learn state machines models from streaming data, showcasing improvements in runtime, memory usage, and result quality compared to existing methods, supported by PAC-framework analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addressing the limitation of existing algorithms that assume all data is available upfront, while concentrating on learning state machines from streaming data.

Method: Introduced a generic learning approach utilizing sketches for incomplete prefix trees, implemented in an open-source state merging library and contrasted against existing techniques.

Result: Demonstrated superior runtime, memory efficiency, and result quality using a popular dataset, along with a formal proof of PAC-learnability.

Conclusion: The approach proves effective and theoretically sound in learning state machines from data streams with additional technical and heuristic enhancements.

Abstract: This is an extended version of our publication Learning state machines from data streams: A generic strategy and an improved heuristic, International Conference on Grammatical Inference (ICGI) 2023, Rabat, Morocco. It has been extended with a formal proof on PAC-bounds, and the discussion and analysis of a similar approach has been moved from the appendix and is now a full Section.
  State machines models are models that simulate the behavior of discrete event systems, capable of representing systems such as software systems, network interactions, and control systems, and have been researched extensively. The nature of most learning algorithms however is the assumption that all data be available at the beginning of the algorithm, and little research has been done in learning state machines from streaming data. In this paper, we want to close this gap further by presenting a generic method for learning state machines from data streams, as well as a merge heuristic that uses sketches to account for incomplete prefix trees. We implement our approach in an open-source state merging library and compare it with existing methods. We show the effectiveness of our approach with respect to run-time, memory consumption, and quality of results on a well known open dataset. Additionally, we provide a formal analysis of our algorithm, showing that it is capable of learning within the PAC framework, and show a theoretical improvement to increase run-time, without sacrificing correctness of the algorithm in larger sample sizes.

</details>


<div id='cs.CE'></div>

# cs.CE [[Back]](#toc)

### [445] [Macroscopic transport patterns of UAV traffic in 3D anisotropic wind fields: A constraint-preserving hybrid PINN-FVM approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01327)
*Hanbing Liang,Fujun Liu*

Main category: cs.CE

TL;DR: This paper addresses challenges in 3D UAV traffic organization under winds and obstacles by proposing a hybrid solver combining physics-based learning with numerical methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The effective organization of UAV traffic in 3D airspace is hindered by anisotropic influences like winds and the need to maintain transport consistency and boundary semantics, areas where traditional approaches fall short.

Method: The proposed method involves a hybrid solver combining a physics-informed neural network (PINN) tackling anisotropic Eikonal problems with a finite-volume method for density transport. These are coupled via Picard iteration with strict conservative boundary conditions.

Result: The framework demonstrated efficacy in capturing critical UAV traffic features such as anisotropic value slices, motion patterns, and steady density structures in different scenarios.

Conclusion: The study highlights the utility of integrating physics-based models with conservative numerical solvers in organizing UAV traffic and stresses the importance of reproducible frameworks for better diagnostics and traceable traffic analysis.

Abstract: Macroscopic unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) traffic organization in three-dimensional airspace faces significant challenges from static wind fields and complex obstacles. A critical difficulty lies in simultaneously capturing the strong anisotropy induced by wind while strictly preserving transport consistency and boundary semantics, which are often compromised in standard physics-informed learning approaches. To resolve this, we propose a constraint-preserving hybrid solver that integrates a physics-informed neural network for the anisotropic Eikonal value problem with a conservative finite-volume method for steady density transport. These components are coupled through an outer Picard iteration with under-relaxation, where the target condition is hard-encoded and strictly conservative no-flux boundaries are enforced during the transport step. We evaluate the framework on reproducible homing and point-to-point scenarios, effectively capturing value slices, induced-motion patterns, and steady density structures such as bands and bottlenecks. Ultimately, our perspective emphasizes the value of a reproducible computational framework supported by transparent empirical diagnostics to enable the traceable assessment of macroscopic traffic phenomena.

</details>


### [446] [Bias Inheritance in Neural-Symbolic Discovery of Constitutive Closures Under Function-Class Mismatch](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01335)
*Hanbing Liang,Ze Tao,Fujun Liu*

Main category: cs.CE

TL;DR: This study proposes a three-phase neural-symbolic framework to robustly recover diffusion and reaction laws in nonlinear reaction-diffusion systems while avoiding common pitfalls in inverse problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in accurately recovering physical laws from data in nonlinear reaction-diffusion systems and to highlight the inadequacy of relying solely on low residuals for validation.

Method: The paper introduces a three-stage neural-symbolic approach: learning surrogates under physical constraints, compressing them into interpretable symbolic forms, and validating symbolic closures via forward re-simulation on new data.

Result: Numerical experiments reveal that classical estimators work well under matched conditions but neural surrogates excel in mismatched scenarios, although symbolic compression inherits biases from neural surrogates.

Conclusion: The true bottleneck lies in solving the numerical inverse problem, not in symbolic compression, and forward validation is crucial for rigorous constitutive claims.

Abstract: We investigate the data-driven discovery of constitutive closures in nonlinear reaction-diffusion systems with known governing PDE structures. Our objective is to robustly recover diffusion and reaction laws from spatiotemporal observations while avoiding the common pitfall where low residuals or short-horizon predictions are conflated with physical recovery. We propose a three-stage neural-symbolic framework: (1) learning numerical surrogates under physical constraints using a noise-robust weak-form-driven objective; (2) compressing these surrogates into restricted interpretable symbolic families (e.g., polynomial, rational, and saturation forms); and (3) validating the symbolic closures through explicit forward re-simulation on unseen initial conditions. Extensive numerical experiments reveal two distinct regimes. Under matched-library settings, weak polynomial baselines behave as correctly specified reference estimators, showing that neural surrogates do not uniformly outperform classical bases. Conversely, under function-class mismatch, neural surrogates provide necessary flexibility and can be compressed into compact symbolic laws with minimal rollout degradation. However, we identify a critical "bias inheritance" mechanism where symbolic compression does not automatically repair constitutive bias. Across various observation regimes, the true error of the symbolic closure closely tracks that of the neural surrogate, yielding a bias inheritance ratio near one. These findings demonstrate that the primary bottleneck in neural-symbolic modeling lies in the initial numerical inverse problem rather than the subsequent symbolic compression. We underscore that constitutive claims must be rigorously supported by forward validation rather than residual minimization alone.

</details>


<div id='cs.HC'></div>

# cs.HC [[Back]](#toc)

### [447] [AromaGen: Interactive Generation of Rich Olfactory Experiences with Multimodal Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01650)
*Yunge Wen,Awu Chen,Jianing Yu,Jas Brooks,Hiroshi Ishii,Paul Pu Liang*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: AromaGen introduces an AI-powered wearable for real-time aroma creation using text or visual inputs, achieving strong user satisfaction in controlled studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limitations of fixed-scent cartridges and the lack of large-scale olfactory datasets in current olfactory interfaces.

Method: A wearable device utilizing a multimodal large language model to convert semantic inputs into structured aroma mixtures, allowing iterative refinement via natural language feedback.

Result: AromaGen achieved high similarity to real food aromas in user evaluations, reducing artificiality and demonstrating superior performance to human-composed mixtures.

Conclusion: AromaGen enables real-time, interactive aroma generation, paving the way for innovative uses in communication, wellbeing, and immersive technologies.

Abstract: Smell's deep connection with food, memory, and social experience has long motivated researchers to bring olfaction into interactive systems. Yet most olfactory interfaces remain limited to fixed scent cartridges and pre-defined generation patterns, and the scarcity of large-scale olfactory datasets has further constrained AI-based approaches. We present AromaGen, an AI-powered wearable interface capable of real-time, general-purpose aroma generation from free-form text or visual inputs. AromaGen is powered by a multimodal LLM that leverages latent olfactory knowledge to map semantic inputs to structured mixtures of 12 carefully selected base odorants, released through a neck-worn dispenser. Users can iteratively refine generated aromas through natural language feedback via in-context learning. Through a controlled user study ($N = 26$), AromaGen matches human-composed mixtures in zero-shot generation and significantly surpasses them after iterative refinement, achieving a median similarity of 8/10 to real food aromas and reducing perceived artificiality to levels comparable to real food. AromaGen is a step towards real-world interactive aroma generation, opening new possibilities for communication, wellbeing, and immersive technologies.

</details>


### [448] [Impact of Multimodal and Conversational AI on Learning Outcomes and Experience](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.02221)
*Karan Taneja,Anjali Singh,Ashok K. Goel*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper explores multimodal and conversational AI (MLLMs) for multimedia learning in STEM, finding that a combination of text and image responses leads to better learning outcomes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how conversational and multimodal AI systems impact learning in visually-rich STEM domains, where their potential is underexplored.

Method: Conducted a randomized controlled online study with 124 participants comparing three approaches to learning biology using AI and traditional methods.

Result: The multimodal conversational AI (MuDoC) yielded the highest learning outcomes, while the text-only AI (TexDoC) had the lowest post-test scores despite being perceived as engaging.

Conclusion: Conversationality reduces extraneous cognitive load while multimodality increases germane load, boosting learning. However, conversational AI alone may lead to inflated perceived understanding without improved learning.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) offer an opportunity to support multimedia learning through conversational systems grounded in educational content. However, while conversational AI is known to boost engagement, its impact on learning in visually-rich STEM domains remains under-explored. Moreover, there is limited understanding of how multimodality and conversationality jointly influence learning in generative AI systems. This work reports findings from a randomized controlled online study (N = 124) comparing three approaches to learning biology from textbook content: (1) a document-grounded conversational AI with interleaved text-and-image responses (MuDoC), (2) a document-grounded conversational AI with text-only responses (TexDoC), and (3) a textbook interface with semantic search and highlighting (DocSearch). Learners using MuDoC achieved the highest post-test scores and reported the most positive learning experience. Notably, while TexDoC was rated as significantly more engaging and easier to use than DocSearch, it led to the lowest post-test scores, revealing a disconnect between student perceptions and learning outcomes. Interpreted through the lens of the Cognitive Load Theory, these findings suggest that conversationality reduces extraneous load, while visual-verbal integration induced by multimodality increases germane load, leading to better learning outcomes. When conversationality is not complemented by multimodality, reduced cognitive effort may instead inflate perceived understanding without improving learning outcomes.

</details>


<div id='eess.IV'></div>

# eess.IV [[Back]](#toc)

### [449] [OkanNet: A Lightweight Deep Learning Architecture for Classification of Brain Tumor from MRI Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01264)
*Okan Uçar,Murat Kurt*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: The paper compares two Deep Learning approaches, OkanNet (custom CNN) and ResNet-50 (Transfer Learning), for brain tumor classification in MRI images, finding ResNet-50 to be more accurate and OkanNet faster and computationally efficient.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for an automated, accurate, and efficient method to analyze MRI images for brain tumor diagnosis, reducing radiologist fatigue and minimizing errors.

Method: Developed a custom CNN architecture ('OkanNet') for efficiency, and used Transfer Learning with ResNet-50 for accuracy; tested both methods on a dataset of 7,023 MRI images.

Result: ResNet-50 achieved 96.49% accuracy and higher precision, while OkanNet achieved 88.10% accuracy but trained approximately 3.2 times faster, favoring mobile systems with limited computational resources.

Conclusion: The study highlights the performance trade-offs between accuracy and computational efficiency in brain tumor classification, with ResNet-50 suitable for high performance and OkanNet viable for systems with limited resources.

Abstract: Medical imaging techniques, especially Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), are accepted as the gold standard in the diagnosis and treatment planning of neurological diseases. However, the manual analysis of MRI images is a time-consuming process for radiologists and is prone to human error due to fatigue. In this study, two different Deep Learning approaches were developed and analyzed comparatively for the automatic detection and classification of brain tumors (Glioma, Meningioma, Pituitary, and No Tumor). In the first approach, a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture named "OkanNet", which has a low computational cost and fast training time, was designed from scratch. In the second approach, the Transfer Learning method was applied using the 50-layer ResNet-50 [1] architecture, pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset. In experiments conducted on an extended dataset compiled by Masoud Nickparvar containing a total of $7,023$ MRI images, the Transfer Learning-based ResNet-50 model exhibited superior classification performance, achieving $96.49\%$ Accuracy and $0.963$ Precision. In contrast, the custom OkanNet architecture reached an accuracy rate of $88.10\%$; however, it proved to be a strong alternative for mobile and embedded systems with limited computational power by yielding results approximately $3.2$ times faster ($311$ seconds) than ResNet-50 in terms of training time. This study demonstrates the trade-off between model depth and computational efficiency in medical image analysis through experimental data.

</details>


### [450] [DenOiS: Dual-Domain Denoising of Observation and Solution in Ultrasound Image Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.02105)
*Can Deniz Bezek,Orcun Goksel*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: DenOiS is a dual-domain denoising framework for medical imaging, enhancing image reconstruction under noisy observations and inexact models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing medical imaging methods struggle with noisy and incomplete measurements, and regularization approaches depend on noise assumptions, creating a need for robust alternatives.

Method: DenOiS employs observation refinement for noise correction and compensates for model simplifications, alongside a diffusion-based plug-and-play reconstruction approach.

Result: DenOiS achieves high-fidelity image reconstruction in challenging quantitative ultrasound scenarios, generalized to real-world data after training in simulations.

Conclusion: DenOiS advances imaging reconstruction robustness and fidelity, addressing critical challenges in medical imaging with noisy and inaccurate inputs.

Abstract: Medical imaging aims to recover underlying tissue properties, using inexact (simplified/linearized) imaging models and often from inaccurate and incomplete measurements. Analytical reconstruction methods rely on hand-crafted regularization, sensitive to noise assumptions and parameter tuning. Among deep learning alternatives, plug-and-play (PnP) approaches learn regularization while incorporating imaging physics during inference, outperforming purely data-driven methods. The performance of all these approaches, however, still strongly depends on measurement quality and imaging model accuracy. In this work, we propose DenOiS, a framework that denoises both input observations and resulting solution in their respective domains. It consists of an observation refinement strategy that corrects degraded measurements while compensating for imaging model simplifications, and a diffusion-based PnP reconstruction approach that remains robust under missing measurements. DenOiS enables generalization to real data from training only in simulations, resulting in high-fidelity image reconstruction with noisy observations and inexact imaging models. We demonstrate this for speed-of-sound imaging as a challenging setting of quantitative ultrasound image reconstruction.

</details>


<div id='physics.optics'></div>

# physics.optics [[Back]](#toc)

### [451] [Enhanced Polarization Locking in VCSELs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01857)
*Zifeng Yuan,Dewen Zhang,Lei Shi,Yutong Liu,Aaron Danner*

Main category: physics.optics

TL;DR: This paper explores polarization dynamics in optical injection locking (OIL) for VCSELs, demonstrating reduced injection power requirements and expanded locking range through tailored oxide aperture designs and bias current tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to overcome the limitations of VCSELs, particularly polarization preference and limited polarization switchability, to enable novel applications like polarization-encoded Ising computers.

Method: The authors designed VCSELs with tailored oxide apertures, adjusted via bias current tuning, and analyzed polarization locking experimentally and through the spin-flip model (SFM).

Result: Experimental findings show a significant reduction in the injection power required (as low as 3.6 μW) and an expanded polarization locking range, supported by coherence with SFM-based analysis.

Conclusion: This work achieves practical improvements in polarization locking of VCSELs, making them more suitable for advanced computational applications such as Ising computers.

Abstract: While optical injection locking (OIL) of vertical-cavity surface-emitting lasers (VCSELs) has been widely studied in the past, the polarization dynamics of OIL have received far less attention. Recent studies suggest that polarization locking via OIL could enable novel computational applications such as polarization-encoded Ising computers. However, the inherent polarization preference and limited polarization switchability of VCSELs hinder their use for such purposes. To address these challenges, we fabricate VCSELs with tailored oxide aperture designs and combine these with bias current tuning to study the overall impact on polarization locking. Experimental results demonstrate that this approach reduces the required injection power (to as low as 3.6 μW) and expands the locking range. To investigate the impact of the approach, the spin-flip model (SFM) is used to analyze the effects of amplitude anisotropy and bias current on polarization locking, demonstrating strong coherence with experimental results.

</details>


<div id='math.OC'></div>

# math.OC [[Back]](#toc)

### [452] [When does learning pay off? A study on DRL-based dynamic algorithm configuration for carbon-aware scheduling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01886)
*Andrea Mencaroni,Robbert Reijnen,Yingqian Zhang,Dieter Claeys*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: The paper explores the use of deep reinforcement learning (DRL) for dynamically configuring algorithms in the carbon-aware permutation flow-shop scheduling problem. It demonstrates robust generalization and performance improvement of DRL policies beyond trained instances compared to static tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of achieving robust and adaptive algorithm configurations for real-world optimization problems through DRL and assess the conditions under which its computational cost is justified.

Method: A DRL-based dynamic algorithm configuration framework was developed and trained on small, simple instances, then evaluated on unseen and more complex instances compared to a static tuned baseline.

Result: DRL policies exhibited strong generalization to unseen scenarios, outperforming static configurations in complex cases while showing comparable performance on simpler instances.

Conclusion: DRL proves to be a promising tool for dynamic algorithm configuration, offering robust generalization and effectiveness, thus justifying its computational expense in dynamic and complex problem settings.

Abstract: Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) has recently emerged as a promising tool for Dynamic Algorithm Configuration (DAC), enabling evolutionary algorithms to adapt their parameters online rather than relying on static tuned configurations. While DRL can learn effective control policies, training is computationally expensive. This cost may be justified if learned policies generalize, allowing the training effort to transfer across instance types and problem scales. Yet, for real-world optimization problems, it remains unclear whether this promise holds in practice and under which conditions the investment in learning pays off. In this work, we investigate this question in the context of the carbon-aware permutation flow-shop scheduling problem. We develop a DRL-based DAC framework and train it exclusively on small, simple instances. We then deploy the learned policy on both similar and more complex unseen instances and compare its performance against a static tuned baseline, which provides a fair point of comparison. Our findings show that the proposed method provides a strong dynamic algorithm control policy that can be effectively transferred to different unseen problem instances. Notably, on simple and cheap to compute instances, similar to those observed during training and tuning, DRL performs comparably with the statically tuned baseline. However, as instance characteristics diverge and computational complexities increase, the DRL-learned policy continuously outperforms static tuning. These results confirm that DRL can acquire robust and generalizable control policies which are effective beyond the training instance distributions. This ability to generalize across instance types makes the initial computational investment worthwhile, particularly in settings where static tuning struggles to adapt to changing problem scenarios.

</details>


### [453] [The Newton-Muon Optimizer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01472)
*Zhehang Du,Weijie Su*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new optimization method, Newton-Muon, derived from the design principles behind the Muon optimizer, which achieves better training efficiency for GPT-2 compared to the standard Muon optimizer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The existing Muon optimizer shows strong performance in training language models, yet its design principles, particularly regarding matrix-gradient orthogonalization, are not well-understood. This work seeks to demystify these principles and propose further improvements.

Method: The authors introduce a surrogate model to approximate the loss function as a quadratic of weight perturbation using three key matrices: gradient, output-space curvature, and the input data. By minimizing this surrogate and applying matrix decomposition, they derive a closed-form update rule, which leads to the Newton-Muon optimizer.

Result: Newton-Muon outperforms the standard Muon optimizer in GPT-2 pretraining by achieving the target validation loss with 6% fewer iteration steps and reducing wall-clock training time by 4%.

Conclusion: Newton-Muon provides a theoretical interpretation of the Muon optimizer as an implicit Newton method and demonstrates empirical training efficiency improvements. This suggests that deeper insights into optimizer design can lead to practical advancements.

Abstract: The Muon optimizer has received considerable attention for its strong performance in training large language models, yet the design principle behind its matrix-gradient orthogonalization remains largely elusive. In this paper, we introduce a surrogate model that not only sheds new light on the design of Muon, but more importantly leads to a new optimizer. In the same spirit as the derivation of Newton's method, the surrogate approximates the loss as a quadratic function of the perturbation to a weight matrix $W$ using only three matrices: the gradient $G$, an output-space curvature matrix $H$, and the data matrix $Z$ that stacks the layer inputs. By minimizing this surrogate in one step and adopting a certain isotropic assumption on the weights, we obtain the closed-form update rule (up to momentum and weight decay) $W \leftarrow W - η\cdot \mathrm{msgn}(G(ZZ^\top)^{-1})$, where $η$ is the learning rate and $\mathrm{msgn}(X)=UV^\top$ if $X=USV^\top$ is a compact singular value decomposition. This new optimization method, which we refer to as Newton-Muon, shows that standard Muon can be interpreted as an implicit Newton-type method that neglects the right preconditioning induced by the input second moment. Empirically, on a reproduction of the earliest publicly released Modded-NanoGPT speedrun configuration using Muon for GPT-2 pretraining, Newton-Muon reaches the target validation loss in 6\% fewer iteration steps and reduces wall-clock training time by about 4\%.

</details>


<div id='physics.comp-ph'></div>

# physics.comp-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [454] [Experimental Design for Missing Physics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01231)
*Arno Strouwen,Sebastián Micluţa-Câmpeanu*

Main category: physics.comp-ph

TL;DR: The paper introduces a method combining experimental design with machine learning to discover missing physics in process systems, tested on a bioreactor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Knowledge of model structures in process systems is often incomplete, necessitating learning missing physics from experimental data.

Method: Combines universal differential equations with symbolic regression and proposes a sequential experimental design technique to optimize data collection for discovering missing physics.

Result: The developed technique was successfully applied to a bioreactor system, recovering missing physics.

Conclusion: The methodology demonstrates the potential for improved discovery of missing physics in process systems using a strategic combination of experimentation and machine learning.

Abstract: For most process systems, knowledge of the model structure is incomplete. This missing physics must then be learned from experimental data. Recently, a combination of universal differential equations and symbolic regression has become a popular tool to discover these missing physics. Universal differential equations employ neural networks to represent missing parts of the model structure, and symbolic regression aims to make these neural networks interpretable. These machine learning techniques require high-quality data to successfully recover the true model structure. To gather such informative data, a sequential experimental design technique is developed which is based on optimally discriminating between the plausible model structures suggested by symbolic regression. This technique is then applied to discovering the missing physics of a bioreactor.

</details>


### [455] [Gradient estimators for parameter inference in discrete stochastic kinetic models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.02121)
*Ludwig Burger,Annalena Kofler,Lukas Heinrich,Ulrich Gerland*

Main category: physics.comp-ph

TL;DR: This work explores three gradient estimators for parameter inference in stochastic kinetic models using the Gillespie SSA, highlighting their complementary strengths and weaknesses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Parameter inference for stochastic kinetic models is challenging because stochastic simulation algorithms, like the Gillespie algorithm, involve non-differentiable operations, limiting the application of traditional gradient-based methods.

Method: Three gradient estimation methods—Gumbel-Softmax Straight-Through (GS-ST), the Score Function estimator, and the Alternative Path estimator—are applied to the Gillespie SSA, and their performance is compared on systems with relaxation and oscillatory dynamics.

Result: The GS-ST estimator generally provides well-behaved gradients but fails in regimes with high parameter sensitivity, whereas the Score Function and Alternative Path estimators yield more robust results with lower variance in challenging settings.

Conclusion: Gradient-based parameter inference can be successfully applied to stochastic kinetic models using the Gillespie algorithm, with different gradient estimators proving useful in varying contexts.

Abstract: Stochastic kinetic models are ubiquitous in physics, yet inferring their parameters from experimental data remains challenging. In deterministic models, parameter inference often relies on gradients, as they can be obtained efficiently through automatic differentiation. However, these tools cannot be directly applied to stochastic simulation algorithms (SSA) such as the Gillespie algorithm, since sampling from a discrete set of reactions introduces non-differentiable operations. In this work, we adopt three gradient estimators from machine learning for the Gillespie SSA: the Gumbel-Softmax Straight-Through (GS-ST) estimator, the Score Function estimator, and the Alternative Path estimator. We compare the properties of all estimators in two representative systems exhibiting relaxation or oscillatory dynamics, where the latter requires gradient estimation of time-dependent objective functions. We find that the GS-ST estimator mostly yields well-behaved gradient estimates, but exhibits diverging variance in challenging parameter regimes, resulting in unsuccessful parameter inference. In these cases, the other estimators provide more robust, lower variance gradients. Our results demonstrate that gradient-based parameter inference can be integrated effectively with the Gillespie SSA, with different estimators offering complementary advantages.

</details>


<div id='cs.LO'></div>

# cs.LO [[Back]](#toc)

### [456] [Compositional Program Verification with Polynomial Functors in Dependent Type Theory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01303)
*C. B. Aberlé*

Main category: cs.LO

TL;DR: This paper introduces a compositional program verification framework using polynomial functors, kleisli morphisms, and dependent polynomials in dependent type theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the need for a compositional and formalized approach to program verification by leveraging categorical structures and dependent type theory.

Method: The framework uses polynomial functors as program interfaces, kleisli morphisms for implementations, dependent polynomials for specifying pre/postconditions, and wiring diagrams / Mealy machines for compositional operational semantics. It employs monoidal functors and transformations in abstract categorical structures.

Result: The framework demonstrates compositional verification capabilities and has been formalized as a proof-of-concept in Agda.

Conclusion: The approach opens avenues for extensions to concurrency, relational verification, and applicability to other categories. It provides a solid categorical foundation for compositional program verification.

Abstract: We present a framework for compositional program verification based on polynomial functors in dependent type theory. In this framework, polynomial functors serve as program interfaces, Kleisli morphisms for the free monad monad serve as implementations, and dependent polynomials encode pre/postcondition specifications. We show that implementations and their verifications compose via wiring diagrams, and that Mealy machines provide a compositional coalgebraic operational semantics. We identify the abstract categorical structure underlying this compositionality as a monoidal functor from specifications to interfaces with a compatible monoidal natural transformation of lax monoidal presheaves; this opens the door to generalizations to other categories, monoidal products, etc., including settings for concurrency and relational verification, which we sketch. As a proof-of-concept, the entire framework has been formalized in Agda.

</details>


### [457] [Type-Checked Compliance: Deterministic Guardrails for Agentic Financial Systems Using Lean 4 Theorem Proving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01483)
*Devakh Rashie,Veda Rashi*

Main category: cs.LO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a solution called Lean-Agent Protocol, which uses formal verification to ensure AI in financial services complies with regulatory standards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap where existing AI guardrail systems fail to meet the strict compliance requirements in financial services due to their probabilistic nature.

Method: A neural-symbolic model, Aristotle, converts institutional policies into Lean 4 code, treating AI actions as mathematical conjectures that require proof for compliance before execution.

Result: The Lean-Agent Protocol achieves mathematically verifiable compliance with financial regulations and operates with cryptographic-level certainty and microsecond latency.

Conclusion: The proposed architecture ensures stringent regulatory compliance for autonomous AI in finance, with a planned three-phase deployment roadmap.

Abstract: The rapid evolution of autonomous, agentic artificial intelligence within financial services has introduced an existential architectural crisis: large language models (LLMs) are probabilistic, non-deterministic systems operating in domains that demand absolute, mathematically verifiable compliance guarantees. Existing guardrail solutions -- including NVIDIA NeMo Guardrails and Guardrails AI -- rely on probabilistic classifiers and syntactic validators that are fundamentally inadequate for enforcing complex multi-variable regulatory constraints mandated by the SEC, FINRA, and OCC. This paper presents the Lean-Agent Protocol, a formal-verification-based AI guardrail platform that leverages the Aristotle neural-symbolic model developed by Harmonic AI to auto-formalize institutional policies into Lean 4 code. Every proposed agentic action is treated as a mathematical conjecture: execution is permitted if and only if the Lean 4 kernel proves that the action satisfies pre-compiled regulatory axioms. This architecture provides cryptographic-level compliance certainty at microsecond latency, directly satisfying SEC Rule 15c3-5, OCC Bulletin 2011-12, FINRA Rule 3110, and CFPB explainability mandates. A three-phase implementation roadmap from shadow verification through enterprise-scale deployment is provided.

</details>


<div id='eess.SP'></div>

# eess.SP [[Back]](#toc)

### [458] [Evaluation of gNB Monostatic Sensing for UAV Use Case](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.02205)
*Steve Blandino,Neeraj Varshney,Jian Wang,Jack Chuang,Camillo Gentile,Nada Golmie*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper explores integrated sensing and communications (ISAC) in 3GPP Release 19, focusing on monostatic sensing for UAVs using 5G New Radio (NR) technologies. They developed the 5GNRad simulator for reproducible studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the need for a clear and reproducible implementation of sensing for ISAC scenarios, particularly those involving UAVs, and to build on 3GPP standards and assumptions.

Method: The researchers implemented an end-to-end monostatic sensing chain using 5G NR downlink CP-OFDM waveform and PRS in the UMa-AV scenario, performing multi-target detection and 3D localization.

Result: Achieved 70%+ target detection probability with under 5% false alarm rates, and minimal localization errors (4m vertical, 6m horizontal 90th-percentile error).

Conclusion: Their implementation demonstrates effective sensing performance and provides a basis for reproducible research with their released simulator, 5GNRad.

Abstract: 3GPP Release 19 has initiated the standardization of integrated sensing and communications (ISAC), including a channel model for monostatic sensing, evaluation scenarios, and performance assessment methodologies. These common assumptions provide an important basis for ISAC evaluation, but reproducible end-to-end studies still require a transparent sensing implementation. This paper evaluates 5G New Radio (NR) base station (gNB)-based monostatic sensing for the Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) use case using a 5G NR downlink Cyclic Prefix-Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (CP-OFDM) waveform and positioning reference signals (PRS), following 3GPP Urban Macro-Aerial Vehicle (UMa-AV) scenario assumptions. We present an end-to-end processing chain for multi-target detection and 3D localization, achieving more than 70% detection probability with less than 5% false alarm rate, in the considered scenario. For correctly detected targets, localization errors are on the order of a few meters, with a 90th-percentile error of 4m and 6m in the vertical and horizontal directions, respectively. To support reproducible baseline studies and further research, we release the simulator 5GNRad, which reproduces our evaluation

</details>


### [459] [Interpretable Battery Aging without Extra Tests via Neural-Assisted Physics-based Modelling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01229)
*Yuan Qiu,Wei Li,Wei Zhang,Yi Zhou,Fang Liu,Jianbiao Wang,Zhi Wei Seh*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper introduces IBAM, a neural-assisted physics-based framework that generates interpretable fingerprints of battery aging using routine management logs, addressing the limitations of single-scalar state of health measures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation behind this work is to improve the interpretability of battery health assessments, as conventional state of health (SoH) metrics are single-scalars that fail to reveal diverse battery degradation patterns.

Method: IBAM integrates a physics-based fractional-order equivalent circuit model with a neural network for regression and fingerprint extraction. A bidirectional gated recurrent unit is used to estimate SoH from multi-channel voltage data.

Result: Across various battery lifespan categories, IBAM shows superior fidelity in its physics model and provides insights into degradation mechanisms and fingerprint patterns.

Conclusion: IBAM enhances the interpretability of battery aging assessments, offering practical implications for optimizing battery operation and control.

Abstract: State of health (SoH) is widely used for battery management, but it is a single scalar and offers limited interpretability. Two batteries with similar SoH can exhibit very different degradation behaviors and the lack of interpretability hinders optimal battery operation. In this paper, we propose IBAM for interpretable battery aging modelling with a neural-assisted physics-based framework. IBAM outputs a 2-D aging fingerprint without extra diagnostic tests and uses only routine logs from the battery management system. The fingerprint offers great interpretability by capturing a battery's curve-wide polarization voltage loss and the tail loss near the end-of-discharge. IBAM first creates a physics-based battery model based on a fractional-order equivalent circuit model, and then extracts per-cycle fingerprints from the model using a two-stage least-squares method. IBAM further anchors fingerprints on the SoH axis with physics-guided regression, where the per-cycle SoH is estimated via a bidirectional gated recurrent unit with customized multi-channel voltage features. Across batteries with short-, medium-, and long-lifespans, IBAM consistently yields the best physics model fidelity at different aging stages, and provides clear interpretations of degradation mechanisms and fingerprint patterns about batteries of different lifespans. The resulting fingerprints support interpretable battery health assessment and can inform battery control choices.

</details>


<div id='cs.SI'></div>

# cs.SI [[Back]](#toc)

### [460] [Can LLMs Predict Academic Collaboration? Topology Heuristics vs. LLM-Based Link Prediction on Real Co-authorship Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01379)
*Fan Huang,Munjung Kim*

Main category: cs.SI

TL;DR: The paper evaluates the ability of large language models (LLMs) to predict future scientific collaborations based on author profiles, finding that LLMs outperformed traditional topology heuristics by leveraging metadata instead of graph structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to explore whether LLMs can improve collaboration prediction using author metadata, addressing limitations in traditional topology-based heuristics, particularly in sparse graph settings.

Method: The study uses Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct and evaluates its performance on co-authorship networks from OpenAlex, focusing on different collaboration prediction tasks across historical phases of AI research. It benchmarks against topology heuristics and node2vec baselines.

Result: LLMs outperform topology heuristics in unbalanced evaluation settings, excelling in new-edge predictions and even where traditional methods fail, such as blind spots with no common neighbors. Providing graph features to LLMs degrades performance.

Conclusion: LLMs and topology heuristics capture distinct collaboration signals. While LLMs excel using metadata like research concepts, they should be used complementary to topology mechanisms for overall predictions.

Abstract: Can large language models (LLMs) predict which researchers will collaborate? We study this question through link prediction on real-world co-authorship networks from OpenAlex (9.96M authors, 108.7M edges), evaluating whether LLMs can predict future scientific collaborations using only author profiles, without access to graph structure. Using Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct across three historical eras of AI research, we find that LLMs and topology heuristics capture distinct signals and are strongest in complementary settings. On new-edge prediction under natural class imbalance, the LLM achieves AUROC 0.714--0.789, outperforming Common Neighbors, Jaccard, and Preferential Attachment, with recall up to 92.9\%; under balanced evaluation, the LLM outperforms \emph{all} topology heuristics in every era (AUROC 0.601--0.658 vs.\ best-heuristic 0.525--0.538); on continued edges, the LLM (0.687) is competitive with Adamic-Adar (0.684). Critically, 78.6--82.7\% of new collaborations occur between authors with no common neighbor -- a blind spot where all topology heuristics score zero but the LLM still achieves AUROC 0.652 by reasoning from author metadata alone. A temporal metadata ablation reveals that research concepts are the dominant signal (removing concepts drops AUROC by 0.047--0.084). Providing pre-computed graph features to the LLM \emph{degrades} performance due to anchoring effects, confirming that LLMs and topology methods should operate as separate, complementary channels. A socio-cultural ablation finds that name-inferred ethnicity and institutional country do not predict collaboration beyond topology, reflecting the demographic homogeneity of AI research. A node2vec baseline achieves AUROC comparable to Adamic-Adar, establishing that LLMs access a fundamentally different information channel -- author metadata -- rather than encoding the same structural signal differently.

</details>


<div id='cs.DB'></div>

# cs.DB [[Back]](#toc)

### [461] [GPU-RMQ: Accelerating Range Minimum Queries on Modern GPUs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01811)
*Lara Kreis,Justus Henneberg,Valentin Henkys,Felix Schuhknecht,Bertil Schmidt*

Main category: cs.DB

TL;DR: This paper presents GPU-RMQ, a new hierarchical and hybrid GPU-based approach for efficient range minimum queries, addressing key limitations of memory overhead, index construction time, and query throughput in existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GPU-based approaches for range minimum queries (RMQ) suffer from issues like high memory usage, slow index construction, and reduced query throughput, especially on large input arrays, making them impractical for scalable applications.

Method: The proposed method, GPU-RMQ, constructs a hierarchical range minimum summary in parallel and processes relevant hierarchy levels using optimized massively-parallel scan operations. It uses both ray tracing cores and CUDA cores in a hybrid fashion to handle queries.

Result: GPU-RMQ achieves significantly better performance compared to existing methods, with up to ~8x higher throughput than LCA, ~17x higher throughput than RTXRMQ, and ~4800x higher throughput than optimized CPU-based approaches. It also has a significantly smaller memory footprint and a much faster index construction time.

Conclusion: GPU-RMQ demonstrates that a hierarchical, hybrid GPU-based approach can efficiently tackle the challenges of RMQ, providing substantial improvements in both performance and scalability over state-of-the-art methods.

Abstract: Range minimum queries are frequently used in string processing and database applications including biological sequence analysis, document retrieval, and web search. Hence, various data structures have been proposed for improving their efficiency on both CPUs and GPUs.Recent work has also shown that hardware-accelerated ray tracing on modern NVIDIA RTX graphic cards can be exploited to answer range minimum queries by expressing queries as rays, which are fired into a scene of triangles representing minima of ranges at different granularities. While these approaches are promising, they suffer from at least one of three issues: severe memory overhead, high index construction time, and low query throughput. This renders these methods practically unusable on larger arrays: For example, the state-of-art GPU-based approaches LCA and RTXRMQ exceed the memory capacity of an NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU for input arrays of size >= 2^29. To tackle these problems, in this work, we present a new approach called GPU-RMQ which is based on a hierarchical approach. GPU-RMQ first constructs a hierarchy of range minimum summaries on top of the original array in a highly parallel fashion. For query answering, only the relevant portions of the hierarchy are then processed in an optimized massively-parallel scan operation. Additionally, GPU-RMQ is hybrid in design enabling the use of both ray tracing cores and CUDA cores across different levels of the hierarchy to handle queries. Our experimental evaluation shows that GPU-RMQ outperforms the state-of-the-art approaches in terms of query throughput especially for larger arrays while offering a significantly lower memory footprint and up to two orders-of-magnitude faster index construction. In particular, it achieves up to ~8x higher throughput than LCA, ~17x higher throughput than RTXRMQ, and up to ~4800x higher throughput compared to an optimized CPU-based approach.

</details>


### [462] [Know Your Streams: On the Conceptualization, Characterization, and Generation of Intentional Event Streams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01440)
*Andrea Maldonado,Christian Imenkamp,Hendrik Reiter,Thomas Seidl,Wilhelm Hasselbring,Martin Werner,Agnes Koschmider*

Main category: cs.DB

TL;DR: The paper addresses the gap in Streaming Process Mining (SPM) evaluation practices by proposing a prototype generator for real-world event streams.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve Streaming Process Mining by addressing the shortcomings in evaluating algorithms using static or artificially created event logs.

Method: The authors conducted a review of data stream literature, extended the conceptual foundation for event streams, and developed a prototype generator called Stream of Intent.

Result: The prototype generator effectively produces reproducible and intentional event streams for benchmarking and algorithm adaptation in SPM.

Conclusion: Stream of Intent is a valuable tool that advances the evaluation and development of SPM algorithms, bridging the gap between real-world complexities and algorithm testing.

Abstract: The shift toward IoT-enabled, sensor-driven systems has transformed how operational data is generated, favoring continuous, real-time event streams (ES) over static event logs. This evolution presents new challenges for Streaming Process Mining (SPM), which must cope with out-of-order events, concurrent activities, incomplete cases, and concept drifts. Yet, the evaluation of SPM algorithms remains rooted in outdated practices, relying on static logs or artificially streamified data that fail to reflect the complexities of real-world streams. To address this gap, we first perform a comprehensive review of data stream literature to identify stream characteristics currently not reflected in the SPM community. Next, we use this information to extend the conceptual foundation for ES. Finally, we propose Stream of Intent, a prototype generator to produce ES with specific features. Our evaluation shows excellence in producing reproducible, intentional ES for targeted benchmarking and adaptive algorithm development in SPM.

</details>


<div id='math.CA'></div>

# math.CA [[Back]](#toc)

### [463] [A Determinantal Approach to a Sharp $\ell^1-\ell^\infty-\ell^2$ Norm Inequality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01525)
*Jose Antonio Lara Benitez*

Main category: math.CA

TL;DR: The paper provides a linear-algebraic proof of the inequality \( \|x\|_1\,\|x\|_\infty \le \frac{1+\sqrt{p}}{2}\,\|x\|_2^2 \), which connects three norms and proves the optimality of the constant.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish and optimize an inequality that relates three key norms in finite-dimensional spaces, which has practical importance in optimization and numerical analysis.

Method: The authors use a linear-algebraic approach leveraging the determinantal structure of a family of quadratic forms to prove the inequality.

Result: The inequality is proven with the constant \((1+\sqrt{p})/2\), and the authors demonstrate that this constant is optimal.

Conclusion: This work provides a concise and rigorous proof of the inequality, enhancing understanding of norm relationships and offering potential leverage in applied contexts.

Abstract: We give a short linear--algebraic proof of the inequality \[ \|x\|_1\,\|x\|_\infty \le \frac{1+\sqrt{p}}{2}\,\|x\|_2^2, \] valid for every \(x\in\mathbb{R}^p\). This inequality relates three fundamental norms on finite-dimensional spaces and has applications in optimization and numerical analysis. Our proof exploits the determinantal structure of a parametrized family of quadratic forms, and we show the constant $(1+\sqrt{p})/2$ is optimal.

</details>


<div id='eess.SY'></div>

# eess.SY [[Back]](#toc)

### [464] [Generative Profiling for Soft Real-Time Systems and its Applications to Resource Allocation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01441)
*Georgiy A. Bondar,Abigail Eisenklam,Yifan Cai,Robert Gifford,Tushar Sial,Linh Thi Xuan Phan,Abhishek Halder*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: The paper presents a novel approach for generating fine-grained timing profiles of real-time tasks in varying resource contexts, improving predictability and resource utilization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the need for accurate timing behavior characterization in real-time systems, especially under dynamic resource contexts, which existing methods fail to capture effectively.

Method: The authors propose a generative profiling method leveraging a conditional multi-marginal Schrödinger Bridge (MSB) formulation to create context-dependent timing profiles, even for unmeasured resource allocations.

Result: The approach is validated using real-world benchmarks and proves effective in adaptive resource allocation scenarios for multicore systems.

Conclusion: The proposed method successfully enables improved execution profiling in real-time systems, offering greater accuracy and adaptability compared to traditional methods.

Abstract: Modern real-time systems require accurate characterization of task timing behavior to ensure predictable performance, particularly on complex hardware architectures. Existing methods, such as worst-case execution time analysis, often fail to capture the fine-grained timing behaviors of a task under varying resource contexts (e.g., an allocation of cache, memory bandwidth, and CPU frequency), which is necessary to achieve efficient resource utilization. In this paper, we introduce a novel generative profiling approach that synthesizes context-dependent, fine-grained timing profiles for real-time tasks, including those for unmeasured resource allocations. Our approach leverages a nonparametric, conditional multi-marginal Schrödinger Bridge (MSB) formulation to generate accurate execution profiles for unseen resource contexts, with maximum likelihood guarantees. We demonstrate the efficiency and effectiveness of our approach through real-world benchmarks, and showcase its practical utility in a representative case study of adaptive multicore resource allocation for real-time systems.

</details>


### [465] [Koopman Subspace Pruning in Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Spaces via Principal Vectors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01459)
*Dhruv Shah,Jorge Cortes*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: The study develops Kernel-SPV and Approximate Kernel-SPV algorithms for pruning subspaces in RKHS geometry, enhancing Koopman operator modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve the invariance and predictive accuracy of data-driven Koopman operator models by addressing limitations in subspace alignment in RKHS.

Method: The authors compute principal angles and vectors in RKHS, followed by introducing scalable algorithms like Kernel-SPV using randomized Nystrom approximations.

Result: Simulation results confirm the effectiveness of Kernel-SPV and Approximate Kernel-SPV in refining subspaces for Koopman operator applications.

Conclusion: The proposed algorithms advance subspace pruning in RKHS, bridging a gap in Koopman operator modeling and improving predictive accuracy.

Abstract: Data-driven approximations of the infinite-dimensional Koopman operator rely on finite-dimensional projections, where the predictive accuracy of the resulting models hinges heavily on the invariance of the chosen subspace. Subspace pruning systematically discards geometrically misaligned directions to enhance this invariance proximity, which formally corresponds to the largest principal angle between the subspace and its image under the operator. Yet, existing techniques are largely restricted to Euclidean settings. To bridge this gap, this paper presents an approach for computing principal angles and vectors to enable Koopman subspace pruning within a Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space (RKHS) geometry. We first outline an exact computational routine, which is subsequently scaled for large datasets using randomized Nystrom approximations. Based on these foundations, we introduce the Kernel-SPV and Approximate Kernel-SPV algorithms for targeted subspace refinement via principal vectors. Simulation results validate our approach.

</details>


### [466] [Neural Robust Control on Lie Groups Using Contraction Methods (Extended Version)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01448)
*Yi Lok Lo,Longhao Qian,Hugh H. T. Liu*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: This paper introduces a learning framework for robust controller design on Lie groups, leveraging neural feedback controllers and robust control contraction metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the challenge of designing robust controllers for systems operating on Lie groups while ensuring geometric constraints are upheld.

Method: A framework combining robust control contraction metrics (RCCM) and neural feedback controllers is proposed, with derived mathematical conditions for ensuring trajectory bounds on Lie group manifolds.

Result: The proposed framework is demonstrated in a quadrotor feedback controller case study, showing improved performance via simulations compared to traditional geometric controllers.

Conclusion: By respecting manifold constraints and using RCCM and neural controllers, the framework offers a promising approach to robust control design, validated through the quadrotor case study.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose a learning framework for synthesizing a robust controller for dynamical systems evolving on a Lie group. A robust control contraction metric (RCCM) and a neural feedback controller are jointly trained to enforce contraction conditions on the Lie group manifold. Sufficient conditions are derived for the existence of such an RCCM and neural controller, ensuring that the geometric constraints imposed by the manifold structure are respected while establishing a disturbance-dependent tube that bounds the output trajectories. As a case study, a feedback controller for a quadrotor is designed using the proposed framework. Its performance is evaluated using numerical simulations and compared with a geometric controller.

</details>


### [467] [MorphoGuard: A Morphology-Based Whole-Body Interactive Motion Controller](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01517)
*Chenjin Wang,Zheng Yan,Yanmin Zhou,Runjie Shen,Bin He*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: This paper introduces a morphology-constrained whole-body control (WBC) approach (MorphoGuard) to enable robots to handle complex multi-contact tasks in dynamic settings, achieving high accuracy in interactive control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots face challenges in managing dynamic multi-contact combinations along a single kinematic chain, such as pushing a door with an elbow while holding an object, due to complex contact representation and joint coupling.

Method: The authors propose MorphoGuard, a morphology-constrained WBC network trained on a dual-arm physical and simulation platform, and conduct experiments to determine optimal model architecture, fusion strategies, and scale.

Result: The proposed method demonstrated a high control accuracy with a contact point management error of approximately 1 cm in multi-object interaction tasks, highlighting its effectiveness.

Conclusion: MorphoGuard proves to be a robust solution for whole-body interactive robot control, handling arbitrary contact combinations with precision and effectiveness in dynamic tasks.

Abstract: Whole-body control (WBC) has demonstrated significant advantages in complex interactive movements of high-dimensional robotic systems. However, when a robot is required to handle dynamic multi-contact combinations along a single kinematic chain-such as pushing open a door with its elbow while grasping an object-it faces major obstacles in terms of complex contact representation and joint configuration coupling. To address this, we propose a new control approach that explicitly manages arbitrary contact combinations, aiming to endow robots with whole-body interactive capabilities. We develop a morphology-constrained WBC network (MorphoGuard)-which is trained on a self-constructed dual-arm physical and simulation platform. A series of model recommendation experiments are designed to systematically investigate the impact of backbone architecture, fusion strategy, and model scale on network performance. To evaluate the control performance, we adopt a multi-object interaction task as the benchmark, requiring the model to simultaneously manipulate multiple target objects to specified positions. Experimental results show that the proposed method achieves a contact point management error of approximately 1 cm, demonstrating its effectiveness in whole-body interactive control.

</details>


### [468] [Global Geometry of Orthogonal Foliations in the Control Allocation of Signed-Quadratic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01912)
*Antonio Franchi*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: This paper analyzes the differential topology of redundancy resolution governed by signed-quadratic actuation maps and proposes a geometric framework for better actuator space utilization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address inefficiencies and singularities in traditional redundancy resolution and control allocation strategies for systems with signed-quadratic actuation maps.

Method: The paper leverages formal topology to establish the nonlinear actuation null-space, stratify actuator orthants into layers, and introduce orthogonal manifold-based allocators avoiding singularities.

Result: The study proves that classical allocation approaches induce singularities in task spaces, while orthogonal manifold-based methods yield fewer sectors, avoiding singularities or even forming a single diffeomorphism in special cases.

Conclusion: The proposed methods improve control allocation for propeller-driven systems, offering a framework to avoid geometric rank loss and singularities, and refining redundancy management.

Abstract: This work formalizes the differential topology of redundancy resolution for systems governed by signed-quadratic actuation maps. By analyzing the minimally redundant case, the global topology of the continuous fiber bundle defining the nonlinear actuation null-space is established. The distribution orthogonal to these fibers is proven to be globally integrable and governed by an exact logarithmic potential field. This field foliates the actuator space, inducing a structural stratification of all orthants into transverse layers whose combinatorial sizes follow a strictly binomial progression. Within these layers, adjacent orthants are continuously connected via lower-dimensional strata termed reciprocal hinges, while the layers themselves are separated by boundary hyperplanes, or portals, that act as global sections of the fibers. This partition formally distinguishes extremal and transitional layers, which exhibit fundamentally distinct fiber topologies and foliation properties. Through this geometric framework, classical pseudo-linear static allocation strategies are shown to inevitably intersect singular boundary hyperplanes, triggering infinite-derivative kinetic singularities and fragmenting the task space into an exponential number of singularity-separated sectors. In contrast, allocators derived from the orthogonal manifolds yield continuously differentiable global sections with only a linear number of sectors for transversal layers, or can even form a single global diffeomorphism to the task space in the case of the two extremal layers, thus completely avoiding geometric rank-loss and boundary-crossing singularities. These theoretical results directly apply to the control allocation of propeller-driven architectures, including multirotor UAVs, marine, and underwater vehicles.

</details>


### [469] [Causal Optimal Coupling for Gaussian Input-Output Distributional Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01406)
*Daran Xu,Amirhossein Taghvaei*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: This paper formulates the problem of identifying an optimal coupling between input-output distributions under causality and marginal distribution requirements as a Schrödinger Bridge problem, providing tractable solutions for Gaussian marginals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the problem of discovering optimal couplings in causal dynamical systems, reflecting both temporal causality and prescribed marginal distributions for system identification.

Method: The problem is formulated as a Schrödinger Bridge, optimizing KL divergence while enforcing causality and marginal requirements, with tractable solutions derived for Gaussian scenarios and quadratic cost functions.

Result: The authors provide a fully characterized, computationally efficient Sinkhorn iteration method to solve the formulated problem and demonstrate its convergence for Gaussian marginal cases.

Conclusion: This work offers a theoretical foundation for using causal optimal transport in identifying dynamical systems from distributional data, with practical implications for system modeling and analysis.

Abstract: We study the problem of identifying an optimal coupling between input-output distributional data generated by a causal dynamical system. The coupling is required to satisfy prescribed marginal distributions and a causality constraint reflecting the temporal structure of the system. We formulate this problem as a Schr"odinger Bridge, which seeks the coupling closest - in Kullback-Leibler divergence - to a given prior while enforcing both marginal and causality constraints. For the case of Gaussian marginals and general time-dependent quadratic cost functions, we derive a fully tractable characterization of the Sinkhorn iterations that converges to the optimal solution. Beyond its theoretical contribution, the proposed framework provides a principled foundation for applying causal optimal transport methods to system identification from distributional data.

</details>


### [470] [Computing the Exact Pareto Front in Average-Cost Multi-Objective Markov Decision Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.02196)
*Jiping Luo,Nikolaos Pappas*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: The paper solves average-cost multi-objective Markov decision processes (MOMDPs) by characterizing their exact Pareto front as a continuous, piecewise-linear surface on a convex polytope.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve methods for solving MOMDPs by characterizing their Pareto front, particularly in average-cost scenarios, without resorting to approximations.

Method: The paper mathematically proves that the Pareto front in average-cost MOMDPs is a piecewise-linear surface on a convex polytope, defined by deterministic policies and their convex combinations.

Result: The study identifies all components of the Pareto front geometrically and provides methods to address non-convex problems without solving the underlying MDPs.

Conclusion: Characterizing the exact Pareto front enables solving MOMDPs with higher precision, providing key insights for applications like remote state estimation.

Abstract: Many communication and control problems are cast as multi-objective Markov decision processes (MOMDPs). The complete solution to an MOMDP is the Pareto front. Much of the literature approximates this front via scalarization into single-objective MDPs. Recent work has begun to characterize the full front in discounted or simple bi-objective settings by exploiting its geometry. In this work, we characterize the exact front in average-cost MOMDPs. We show that the front is a continuous, piecewise-linear surface lying on the boundary of a convex polytope. Each vertex corresponds to a deterministic policy, and adjacent vertices differ in exactly one state. Each edge is realized as a convex combination of the policies at its endpoints, with the mixing coefficient given in closed form. We apply these results to a remote state estimation problem, where each vertex on the front corresponds to a threshold policy. The exact Pareto front and solutions to certain non-convex MDPs can be obtained without explicitly solving any MDP.

</details>


### [471] [Neural Network-Assisted Model Predictive Control for Implicit Balancing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.01805)
*Seyed Soroush Karimi Madahi,Kenneth Bruninx,Bert Claessens,Chris Develder*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: The paper presents a method that uses input convex neural networks with attention-based mechanisms to improve decision quality and computational efficiency in model predictive control for balancing energy markets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to improve the accuracy in balancing market models used in model predictive control, addressing shortcomings in existing approaches such as TSOs ignoring sub-quarter-hour dynamics or machine learning not integrating with MPC.

Method: The paper proposes integrating an input convex neural network with attention-based input gating mechanisms into a model predictive control framework to capture uncertainties and ensure computational efficiency.

Result: The evaluation on Belgian data demonstrates improved decision-making quality and reduced computational time for the balancing market model.

Conclusion: The proposed data-driven approach successfully addresses limitations in balancing market modeling, improving grid stability support and market decision efficiency.

Abstract: In Europe, balance responsible parties can deliberately take out-of-balance positions to support transmission system operators (TSOs) in maintaining grid stability and earn profit, a practice called implicit balancing. Model predictive control (MPC) is widely adopted as an effective approach for implicit balancing. The balancing market model accuracy in MPC is critical to decision quality. Previous studies modeled this market using either (i) a convex market clearing approximation, ignoring proactive manual actions by TSOs and the market sub-quarter-hour dynamics, or (ii) machine learning methods, which cannot be directly integrated into MPC. To address these shortcomings, we propose a data-driven balancing market model integrated into MPC using an input convex neural network to ensure convexity while capturing uncertainties. To keep the core network computationally efficient, we incorporate attention-based input gating mechanisms to remove irrelevant data. Evaluating on Belgian data shows that the proposed model both improves MPC decisions and reduces computational time.

</details>


<div id='q-fin.MF'></div>

# q-fin.MF [[Back]](#toc)

### [472] [Reinforcement Learning for Speculative Trading under Exploratory Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.02035)
*Yun Zhao,Alex S. L. Tse,Harry Zheng*

Main category: q-fin.MF

TL;DR: The paper studies speculative trading using exploratory reinforcement learning, with a focus on entry and exit timing optimization. It also develops an RL algorithm applied to a pairs-trading case.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address sequential trading decisions in speculative trading by leveraging the exploratory reinforcement learning framework, improving decision-making under uncertainty.

Method: The problem is modeled as a sequential optimal stopping issue. A relaxed version uses Cox processes and entropy-regularized objectives, leading to exploratory HJB equations and closed-form Gibbs distributions.

Result: The paper establishes error bounds, convergence of the RL method to the original problem's value function, and validates the proposed algorithm through implementation in a pairs-trading setup.

Conclusion: The work successfully formulates and analyzes a speculative trading problem, providing theoretical guarantees and a working RL algorithm for practical applications.

Abstract: We study a speculative trading problem within the exploratory reinforcement learning (RL) framework of Wang et al. [2020]. The problem is formulated as a sequential optimal stopping problem over entry and exit times under general utility function and price process. We first consider a relaxed version of the problem in which the stopping times are modeled by the jump times of Cox processes driven by bounded, non-randomized intensity controls. Under the exploratory formulation, the agent's randomized control is characterized via the probability measure over the jump intensities, and their objective function is regularized by Shannon's differential entropy. This yields a system of the exploratory HJB equations and Gibbs distributions in closed-form as the optimal policy. Error estimates and convergence of the RL objective to the value function of the original problem are established. Finally, an RL algorithm is designed, and its implementation is showcased in a pairs-trading application.

</details>


<div id='cs.IT'></div>

# cs.IT [[Back]](#toc)

### [473] [Best-Arm Identification with Noisy Actuation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.02255)
*Merve Karakas,Osama Hanna,Lin F. Yang,Christina Fragouli*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper explores identifying the best arm in a multi-armed bandit scenario using distributed communication over a discrete memoryless channel (DMC).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in identifying the best arm in multi-armed bandit problems over communication-constrained distributed systems.

Method: Proposing communication schemes linked to the zero-error capacity of the DMC, tailored to the agent's capabilities.

Result: The analysis ties communication performance directly to the mathematical concept of zero-error capacity.

Conclusion: Insights are provided into designing effective communication strategies for distributed multi-armed bandit problems on DMCs.

Abstract: In this paper, we consider a multi-armed bandit (MAB) instance and study how to identify the best arm when arm commands are conveyed from a central learner to a distributed agent over a discrete memoryless channel (DMC). Depending on the agent capabilities, we provide communication schemes along with their analysis, which interestingly relate to the zero-error capacity of the underlying DMC.

</details>


<div id='cs.CY'></div>

# cs.CY [[Back]](#toc)

### [474] [Generative AI Spotlights the Human Core of Data Science: Implications for Education](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.02238)
*Nathan Taback*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: The paper discusses how Generative AI (GAI) emphasizes the importance of human reasoning in data science, even as GAI automates many workflows like cleaning and modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the impact of Generative AI on data science education and emphasize the ongoing importance of uniquely human competencies.

Method: The authors analyze GAI's impact through theoretical frameworks, such as Donoho's Greater Data Science framework, and historical perspectives on the evolution of data science.

Result: GAI automates computing tasks (e.g., GDS3), but human input remains essential for tasks related to problem formulation, ethics, and sensemaking.

Conclusion: Data science education should highlight human reasoning and judgment, teaching students how to collaborate with GAI effectively in iterative workflows, while assessments should reflect these competencies.

Abstract: Generative AI (GAI) reveals an irreducible human core at the center of data science: advances in GAI should sharpen, rather than diminish, the focus on human reasoning in data science education. GAI can now execute many routine data science workflows, including cleaning, summarizing, visualizing, modeling, and drafting reports. Yet the competencies that matter most remain irreducibly human: problem formulation, measurement and design, causal identification, statistical and computational reasoning, ethics and accountability, and sensemaking.
